Automating API Busywork in Scala

Hi all, welcome to my talk, Automating API Busywork in Scala.

I’ve had a lot of fun listening to the latest developments in Scala land.

This talk uses something that is absolutely not latest. One was available since 3.0, one was available since 2.13.

Old stuff. But we use them in production. Likely you haven’t heard about it.

Haemin Yoo · Scalar 2026

Rediscovering Schema as Data

So one layer deeper, I want to talk about this.

I gave a talk last year where I see FP as the paradigm of reification and composition patterns.

This is in the same spirit. A lot of API schema information are implicit. I want to reify, treat them as data, and that makes some things easier to reason about.

The Dragon Rental API

function rentDragon:

(Dragon Id) -> (Rental)

Look at this API. It’s a dragon rental API. You input a dragon ID, and you get a rental object out of it.

What information is associated with an API?

  • Dragon id?

  • Rental object?

Also: Schema

So let’s talk about interfaces.

All interfaces — whether it’s a REST API, a GraphQL API, a Scala function, or even a physical kiosk for ordering food — when you have an interface, what information is associated with it?

The dragon ID in the request, like smaug? The rental object in the response?

Those are right, but they are at the domain level.

Schema is also the related information.

Two types of schema information in this talk.

Part 1

  • first schema element that I find annoying to maintain.

List of possible errors

  1. Understand what errors can happen

  2. Document it somewhere

Can we automate?

Which errors can this call raise?

What do teams usually do to maintain this? They maintain this list manually by hand.

Source of data

Code!

First: where does this come from?

The list of possible error enums is in the code. You read the code, you figure it out, and then you communicate it somehow to the API consumer.

Applicable to REST, GraphQL, GRPC, …​

Goal

// Given...
case class DragonNotFound(id: DragonId)
case class DragonVacationing(id: DragonId)
// Business logic
for {
  _ <- checkDragonExists(id)
  _ <- checkDragonAvailable(id)
} yield Rental(...)

// Get a handle of the error as value or type somehow

Now consider the following errors: DragonNotFound, DragonVacationing.

If you have a code path rentDragon that can raise both, then the code for rentDragon actually has enough information to generate that list.

How do we build this automatically?

You can do it with your eyeballs — that’s the proof it can be done.

But how can we do that automatically, with our favorite tool — the compiler?

Composing with flatMap

// Works with Either, ...
def checkDragonExists(id: DragonId)
  : IO[DragonNotFound, Unit] = ???

def checkDragonAvailable(id: DragonId)
  : IO[DragonVacationing, Unit] = ???
// Part of rentDragon
val check = for {
  _ <- checkDragonExists(id)
  _ <- checkDragonAvailable(id)
} yield ()
IO[DragonNotFound | DragonVacationing, Unit]

Done?

Now, as Scala 3 users, we have a new feature compared to Scala 2: union types.

Let’s say you have two operations: checkDragonExists and checkDragonAvailable, both returning IO[…​, Unit].

If you compose them with flatMap, you get that exact union type.

The advantage of this is that it is always precise, and it requires no manual maintenance.

Problems

But if you follow this pattern, you also run into a problem.

What is it?

Vanilla Case Classes

case class DragonNotFound(id: DragonId)
case class DragonVacationing(id: DragonId)

// Not a Throwable. No stack traces.

You aren’t extending Throwable.

You don’t get stack traces. You don’t get to use the machinery built around Throwable for error handling, like interop with Java code.

Adding Stack Traces Manually

case class DragonNotFound(id: DragonId) {
  val stackTrace = ??? // 😩
}

case class DragonVacationing(id: DragonId) {
  val stackTrace = ??? // Again!!
}

So how do you get stack traces?

Simple: add the code that captures stacktraces into the constructor. Repeat that in every case class you want to treat as an error.

But you don’t want to repeat. How do you abstract over repeated code in the constructor?

That’s right, you extend something. An abstract class or a trait.

Widening

sealed trait Rejection extends RuntimeException

case class DragonNotFound(id: DragonId)
  extends Rejection
case class DragonVacationing(id: DragonId)
  extends Rejection
val check = for {
  _ <- checkDragonExists(id)
  _ <- checkDragonAvailable(id)
} yield ()
IO[Rejection, Unit]

But then you get this problem: the type inference becomes the supertype — in this case, Rejection — not A | B.

So you’re thrown into a dilemma.

Either you keep vanilla case classes, or you accept that union type accumulation doesn’t work.

Quiz

Who knows what’s the solution? Raise of hand. Who knows where I am going?

transparent trait

transparent trait Rejection extends RuntimeException

case class DragonNotFound(id: DragonId)
  extends Rejection
case class DragonVacationing(id: DragonId)
  extends Rejection
val result = for {
  _ <- checkDragonExists(id)
  _ <- checkDragonAvailable(id)
} yield ()
IO[DragonNotFound | DragonVacationing, Unit]

That’s right: transparent traits.

Now you get the best of both worlds. Only in Scala 3.

Stack traces preserved. Union inference preserved.

Harvesting

def rentDragon(id: Dragon.Id) = for {
  // Returns IO
case class Result[E, A](value: ? => IO[E, A]) {
  type Out = E | A
}
val r = Result(rentDragon)
type RentDragonResult = r.Out

You can now refer to the error type as the union type by putting it in a box:

Now you can refer to Out as a path-dependent type, harvest it, and feed it to schema derivation in, say, Caliban.

Always up to date

given Schema[Any, r.Out] = Schema.unionType[r.Out]
union RentDragonResult =
  DragonNotFound | DragonVacationing | Rental

type Query {
  rentDragon(id: String!): RentDragonResult!
}

Auto-generated!

And you will get an up-to-date GraphQL error schema straight from the source.

And even if you’re not using Caliban or GraphQL, it still works.

Exhaustive Pattern Match

result.value.either.map {
  // Keep it up to date with
  // -Wconf:name=PatternMatchExhaustivity:e
  //       ,name=MatchCaseUnreachable:e
  case Left(e: DragonNotFound)     => ???
  case Left(e: DragonVacationing)  => ???
  case Right(rental)               => ???
}

Even if you don’t directly harvest the errors to generate a GraphQL schema,

you can project the effect to Either at the boundary and pattern match on the errors to create error responses.

By failing on non-exhaustive matches and unreachable cases, you get notified when the set of possible errors changes.

Only errors?

  1. Which events can this emit?

  2. Which state does this touch?

  3. What authorization does this require?

The idea probably doesn’t stop at error handling. Anything that’s defined in code, you can infer it.

Error sets, as data

So that was one schema-level concern that I made as data.

Now we get speed of iteration.

We can check the generated schema into Git and have it reviewed by frontend teams or internally to catch breaking changes.

It helps with iteration speed.

Part 2

Now onto my second point.

In part 1, the schema level data was the error set, communicated from the backend code to the audience.

In part 2, the schema level data is a different data we want to communicate in the other direction.

Same move: make it explicit, then propagate it with the right mechanism.

rentDragon(dragonId)

What should it return?

If you have a rentDragon(dragonId) API exposed over the network, conceptually, what should it return?

This is a trick question.

We can’t know!

The client needs to communicate the selection tree to the server

The answer is: we can’t know.

Or rather, the question is underspecified to provide an answer.

What we want to return is orthogonal to the operation itself. It’s a UX concern.

What the client needs dictates what we want to return from that operation.

So what is "what the client needs"? What is the data?

It’s the traversal of the domain graph in the form of trees, starting from a node. In that case, a Rental.

Domain Relations

graph LR Rental ~~~ Dragon Dragon ~~~ Breed Dragon ~~~ Schedule style Rental fill:#fff,stroke:#333 style Dragon fill:#fff,stroke:#ddd,color:#bbb style Breed fill:#fff,stroke:#ddd,color:#bbb style Schedule fill:#fff,stroke:#ddd,color:#bbb
graph LR Rental --> Dragon Dragon ~~~ Breed Dragon ~~~ Schedule style Rental fill:#fff,stroke:#333 style Dragon fill:#fff,stroke:#333 style Breed fill:#fff,stroke:#ddd,color:#bbb style Schedule fill:#fff,stroke:#ddd,color:#bbb linkStyle 0 stroke:#e74c3c,stroke-width:3px
graph LR Rental --> Dragon Dragon --> Breed Dragon ~~~ Schedule style Rental fill:#fff,stroke:#333 style Dragon fill:#fff,stroke:#333 style Breed fill:#fff,stroke:#333 style Schedule fill:#fff,stroke:#ddd,color:#bbb linkStyle 0 stroke:#e74c3c,stroke-width:3px linkStyle 1 stroke:#e74c3c,stroke-width:3px
graph LR Rental --> Dragon Dragon --> Breed Dragon --> Schedule style Rental fill:#fff,stroke:#333 style Dragon fill:#fff,stroke:#333 style Breed fill:#fff,stroke:#333 style Schedule fill:#fff,stroke:#333 linkStyle 0 stroke:#e74c3c,stroke-width:3px linkStyle 1 stroke:#e74c3c,stroke-width:3px linkStyle 2 stroke:#e74c3c,stroke-width:3px

You created a rental, so you can start with Rental.

Then you might want to get the associated Dragon.

Then you might want to get the associated Breed.

What is that association?

It’s a relation.

REST & SQL

So far this has been abstract.

Now let’s pin it to the default architecture most teams know.

Frontend calls a REST endpoint.

The backend then talks to SQL, or a cache, or another service, or whatever else is behind it.

What does backend do?

So what does the backend app actually do?

It takes the shape implicitly requested by the frontend, and turns it into some mix of SQL, cache lookups, RPC calls, or stitching code.

That is where the interesting problem lives.

There are really two selection trees here.

One on the frontend-facing side of the backend: what shape does the REST endpoint return?

And one on the datasource-facing side of the backend: what shape do we fetch from below?

Same intent, duplicated around the backend.

Organizational reasons

Trees in one place!

And this default was not stupid.

It was a good default.

The endpoint shape lived in the backend app.

The SQL or stitching shape lived in the backend app.

So from the organization’s point of view, there was one place to change.

Selection-tree churn

This architecture works well when shapes are stable.

But in a fast-paced environment, they are not stable.

Frontend wants slightly different response shapes.

Product changes screens.

Design moves fields around.

And then the bottleneck is not raw runtime performance.

The bottleneck is engineering time spent updating those hardcoded selection trees.

FE-side selection trees are mostly solved

GraphQL

If the frontend is the bottleneck, there is already a widely known answer.

GraphQL.

It lets the client send the selection tree in-band instead of opening tickets for new shapes.

Solved enough.

Backend selection trees are not

  • Hard to abstract query languages!

  • Multiple sources!

Local resolution

But representing selection trees inside the backend is definitely not a solved problem.

Why? Not through a lack of effort. We already had an excellent talk to abstract over SQL even today.

So much effort gone into abstracting over query languages. In my opinion, it’s far from being conquered.

But it gets unwieldy once the shape becomes dynamic, or the query gets ugly.

If the data lives in multiple places, it just doesn’t work.

My theory: there is no single mainstream declarative abstraction that spans all the kinds of datasources a backend talks to.

Local resolution’s first problem: N+1

  • Bad default: singular fetches

  • Think: singular as specialized case of plural

  • Compose singular & plural fetches in a single call

If we can’t abstract selection trees, why don’t we turn to local resolution?

First problem: N plus one.

And N plus one is not just a bug.

Repository modules tend to expose the minimum thing that solves the current task.

Then application code composes singular fetches in a loop, and you get N plus one almost by default.

N+1 is solved

def getScheduleView(schedules: List[Schedule.Id])
  : Task[List[(Schedule, Dragon)]] =
  // Efficient!
  (for {
    schedules <- getSchedules(dragon.id)
    dragons   <- ZQuery.foreachBatched(
                         schedules.map(s => getDragon(s.dragonId))
                       )
                       .map(d => d.id -> d).toMap
  } yield ScheduleView(
    schedules.map(s => s -> dragons(s.dragonId))
  )).run

Where is the tree?

Facebook ran into that problem hard enough to build Haxl.

Later, the same idea became widely known as DataLoader in JS land.

This problem was universal — in Scala, we have multiple ports, including ZQuery in ZIO and Fetch in cats-effect.

Haxl solved the execution side.

Batching, deduplication, reasonable performance.

But look at this code. Do you see the selection tree?

Local resolution’s second problem: imperative code

Haxl did not solve the representation problem.

You still write the imperative program first.

Each step models a call to a datasource.

Each subroutine is built ad hoc.

The selection tree is still implicit in the control flow.

So even if execution is okay, ergonomics are still bad.

represent the selection tree itself?

What do we actually want to represent?

Not batching logic.

Not imperative control flow.

We want to represent the selection tree itself.

So just express the selection tree as data, and translate it into the imperative program.

decrel

That is exactly what decrel does.

Edges as values, composed into trees

It makes the edges of your domain relation graph into simple values, and lets you compose them into tree shapes.

Now the selection tree is explicit.

You can express it directly as values, rather than indirectly through imperative stitching code, or through a SQL string.

decrel expressions

val schedules: List[Schedules]
def getScheduleView(schedules: List[Schedule.Id])
  : TaskQuery[List[(Schedule, Dragon)]] =
  (Schedule.fetch <>: Schedule.dragon)
    .startingFrom(schedules)
  // List[(Schedule, Dragon, Breed)]
  (Schedule.fetch <>: Schedule.dragon <>: Dragon.breed)
    .startingFrom(schedules)

Now this .startingFrom compiles your tree into the imperative map and flatMap program.

And if you run it with the right monadic type — say ZQuery or Fetch — you get "good enough" performance with manual stitching and a declarative API.

You saw the API.

It’s basically: get the dragon from the rental by following an edge, then get the breed and the vacation schedule of the dragon in parallel by following two more edges.

it’s not magic

given scheduleDragonProof: Proof.Single[
  Schedule.dragon.type,
  Schedule,
  Throwable,
  Dragon
] =
  implementSingleDatasource(Rental.dragon) {
    (schedules: Chunk[Schedule]) =>
      // Implement yourself (efficiently)
      // Given a Chunk[Schedule],
      //   return a Chunk[(Schedule, Dragon)]
  }
val schedule: Schedule = ???
Schedule.dragon.startingFrom(schedule)

// (3.8+)
schedule.expand(Schedule.dragon)

Stitching as the default, SQL as optimization

Now I’m absolutely not claiming that you should never write joins anymore.

Joins are more likely to be faster than manual stitching with ZQuery or Fetch.

What I am claiming, though, is that once local resolution becomes ergonomic, it can be the sane default.

That is the change decrel is aiming for.

If fast UI iteration pushes you toward GraphQL, decrel can sit underneath it too. GraphQL resolvers need to solve the same backend problem.

And if you are on a REST backend and the real bottleneck is selection-set churn inside the backend app, decrel gives you a much nicer local-resolution default than one SQL string per endpoint.

Declarative is not enough

-- Before
SELECT r.id, d.id, b.name, s.starts_at, s.ends_at
FROM rental r
JOIN dragon d ON d.id = r.dragon_id
JOIN breed b ON b.id = d.breed_id
LEFT JOIN schedule s ON s.dragon_id = d.id
WHERE r.id = ?;
-- After
SELECT r.id, d.id, b.name
FROM rental r
JOIN dragon d ON d.id = r.dragon_id
JOIN breed b ON b.id = d.breed_id
WHERE r.id = ?;

See the difference?

Suppose you get a ticket to remove the schedule from the response.

On the SQL side, that often means editing the select list, the joins, and the row decoding logic that sits next to the query.

Even in this toy version, the diff spreads across multiple lines.

In decrel

// Before
(Rental.dragon <>: (Dragon.breed & Dragon.schedule))
  .startingFrom(rental)
// After
(Rental.dragon <>: Dragon.breed)
  .startingFrom(rental)

In decrel, the diff is the tree diff.

You remove one edge from the tree, and the resulting program follows that new shape.

Back to our theme.

The information we want to convey was the selection tree.

Closing

What did we learn?

  1. transparent traits (do try this at home!)

  2. selection trees

Reify implicit ideas into values

So what did you learn?

  1. reifying error sets with transparent traits

  2. reifying selection trees with decrel

Only possible in Scala

  • Deriving error sets

    1. Configurable type inference (transparent)

  • Decrel

    1. Right-associative operators

    2. Term inference (implicit search)

    3. Module-scoped typeclass instances (lexical scope orphaned instances)

    4. Production-ready

And I mean this quite literally: decrel is possible in this exact form because Scala has all the things I need.

Right-associative operators, so the tree shape reads naturally in the code.

Term inference, so you don’t rely on a runtime registry of data fetching implementations.

Module-scoped typeclass instances, so one relation can have multiple implementations with the same typeclass.

Last but not least, it must be practical for mainstream backend application development.

Other languages have parts of these features, so decrel can be replicated in spirit.

But if you want this exact shape and this level of ergonomics, Scala is the only one I know.

Thank you!

Demo code available in

https://github.com/yoohaemin/scalar2026api
